card.intuition: Distributing incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed and the system remains available.
card.analogy: A grocery store with 10 checkout lines: a manager (Load Balancer) stands at the entrance and directs each customer to a line so that no one waits too long in the same queue.
when_to_use: Scaling your application to handle more users than a single server can support
Ensuring High Availability (if one server dies, the load balancer skips it)
Zero-downtime deployments (gradually shifting traffic from old version to new version)
gotchas: Sticky Sessions: if your app stores state on the server (session auth), you must force the Load Balancer to send the same user to the same server every time
L4 (Transport) vs L7 (Application) load balancing: L7 is smarter (can route by URL path) but requires more CPU power

steps.annotation: System overview — step through to trace the flow.
All client traffic arrives at the load balancer.
LB applies algorithm (round-robin, least-conn, IP-hash) and checks health.
Request forwarded to a healthy server. Failed servers removed automatically.

exercise.prompt: Identify the basic load balancing algorithm that cycles through servers in order (1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3...).
hints: It's a common name for a circular rotation
exercise.test_code:
def check(ans): return ans.lower() == "round robin"
assert check("Round Robin")
print("PASS")
